message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "CLI: quote with unknown item type (broomstick) → non-zero exit, error to stderr, no results on stdout"
Expected behavior: when a quote step contains an item whose type is not one of the known types (sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone), the CLI must write an error description to stderr, exit with a non-zero status, and NOT write a results object to stdout.
Implementation approach: validate item types. The cleanest minimal approach: have quote() (or a validation in cli.ts) detect unknown types and throw an Error; wrap the CLI's processing in a try/catch that on error writes the message to stderr (e.g. console.error) and calls process.exit(1) WITHOUT writing results to stdout. Define the known type set. Note: the base-premium table currently maps known types; unknown types should be rejected rather than falling back to DEFAULT_BASE_PREMIUM. Make sure all 39 existing tests still pass (they only use known types).
Current error: expected status non-zero, received 0 (CLI defaults broomstick to base 100, exits 0).
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts and src/cli.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

56-- Components (rune/moonstone) have no enchantment/material → no special clause.
57-- Cap persists across successive claims on the same policy.
58-
59:### R6 — Error cases (CLI: non-zero exit + stderr, no results on stdout)
60:- Unknown item type in a quote (e.g. "broomstick").
61-- Claim references a damage entry whose item is not in the policy
62:  (wrong type, or unknown type).
63-- Damage entry with negative amount (e.g. -200).
64-- More damage entries of a given type than the policy insures.
65-
--
107-- dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G (dragon only: 800 − 100).
108-- steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (high-ench: 500 − 100).
109-
110:### Multiple items of same type (R1, R5, R6)
111-- two swords → insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G.
112-- dragon attack with two sword damage entries → each its own deductible.
113-- more sword damage entries than swords insured → CLI non-zero exit, claim rejected.
--
125-- premium 197.5 G → 198 G (up).
126-- payout 350.5 G → 350 G (down).
127-
128:### Edge cases (R1, R6)
129-- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the fee).
130:- quote with unknown type (broomstick) → CLI non-zero exit + stderr, no results.
131-- claim references item not in policy (amulet damaged, only sword insured) →
132-  CLI non-zero exit + stderr.
133-- claim damage amount -200 → CLI non-zero exit + stderr.
--
230-35. successive claims second 600 cap-remaining 0 — R5 cap exhaustion.
231-36. CLI: reads stdin scenario, writes results to stdout — R7 happy path.
232-37. CLI: quote then claim sequential, claim references policy index — R7.
233:38. CLI error: unknown item type (broomstick) → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
234:39. CLI error: claim item not in policy → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
235:40. CLI error: negative damage amount → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
236:41. CLI error: more damage entries of a type than insured → non-zero exit — R6.

content.text:
I now understand the failing test. The minimal change is to make the CLI reject unknown item types. The current error is that `unmodifiedBasePremiumFor` defaults unknown types to 100 base, so the CLI completes and exits 0.

The minimal approach per the implementation guidance: detect the unknown type, write to stderr, and exit non-zero without writing results. Since only one error test is active (the broomstick quote case), I'll do the smallest change that makes this test pass while keeping all 39 tests green.

The smallest fake: in the CLI, wrap processing in a try/catch and have `quote()` (or the CLI) reject unknown types. The cleanest minimal move is to add a known-type check in `quote()` that throws, and a try/catch in the CLI. But that touches `quote()` which all the unit tests use directly with known types — they won't break.

Let me do the minimal version: add the known-type validation in the CLI itself, since the active test is a CLI test. This keeps `quote()` untouched and is the smaller change.

content.text:
All 40 tests pass (39 prior + the newly activated broomstick test), 3 todo remaining. Let me verify the over-implementation check:

- Did I implement features for future tests? No — the other 3 error cases (claim item not in policy, negative damage, too many damages) are still `it.todo()` and untouched.
- Did I add logic not demanded? No — only the unknown-type quote check.
- Did I optimize prematurely? No.
- Did I refactor existing code? No — `quote()` and `claim()` are untouched.

This is minimal: a `Set` of the six known types and a guard in the quote branch that writes to stderr and exits non-zero before any results are written.

Now appending to the journal.

content.text:
🟢 Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added a `KNOWN_ITEM_TYPES` set (sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone) in `src/cli.ts`. In the `quote` branch of the step loop, the CLI now checks each item's type against that set; on an unknown type it writes an error message to stderr and calls `process.exit(1)` before any results object is written to stdout.

**Result**: All tests now pass (40 passing | 3 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal — the only active error test is the CLI broomstick-quote case, so the guard lives in the CLI's quote branch and touches nothing else. `quote()`, `claim()`, and `claim-office.ts` are entirely untouched, so all 39 prior tests (which use only known types) stay green. The remaining 3 error cases (claim item not in policy, negative damage, too many damage entries) are still `it.todo()` and were not implemented — no anticipation of future tests. No optimization, no refactoring.
